[2019-09-22 22:33] mrguy888: Let me teach you a thing or two about game design, zera.

The Prismata base set is designed in a way to fulfill a very specific job. The base set has the 5 basic resource production units to ensure you can always build every unit. The rest of the base set is a basic attacker and basic defender in each color. This does 2 things. The first thing is that it lets fill any hole you need. No matter what single tech resource you have, you can spend it on permanent attack, or you can spend it on prompt hp.

The second job it does is set a baseline power level. Every set has at least Wall absorb and at least Tarsier attack. These two jobs allows you to make the random set fully random without getting really uncomfortable situations such as not having any damage dealers, or having to buy Forges in doubles because all blue units cost an even number of blues.

It is important that the job is doesn't do is outshine random set units. It wouldn't make sense to construct the base set to maximize how random you can make the advanced set, then make the advanced set units just lose to base set anyway.

That is why there should logically never be counterplay to 2 random set units with only the base set. If there were, either the base set isn't doing its job, or the advanced set isn't doing its job.

Now, Hound and Vore are attackers. The goal of Prismata is to kill your opponent. You need to make swords to do that. That makes buying basic sword units a requirement in every set. The counterplay to attack is absorb, though you can only counter a limited amount per game. Hound and Vore are relatively cheap units, which makes them easy to buy, but are balanced to be weaker than units that have higher cost and force you to jump through some hoops to buy. A 9 drone vore hound rush is losing to Matrix Cynestra, for an obvious extreme example.
[2019-09-22 22:33] mrguy888: The burden of proof is not on everyone else to prove your change bad, but it is on you to describe realistic situations where Hound makes the game imbalanced by the coinflip or buy pushing units out of viability in situations where they logically shouldn't be.

[2019-09-23 03:27] mrguy888: You can look at innervi field to help you. It can be 4gg for 3 now, 4 the turn after, and 5 the turn after that. It even has lifespan. The 3 and 4 hp options are by far the most common with the best one depending on a lot of things. 5 in 3 is not very common at all as it is the least efficient.

6 for 3 next turn is what engis do and, while that is not very efficient, the fact that it is colorless makes it a taken option fairly frequently. 6 for 6 in 2 turns is clearly much too powerful as 4 is typically the most interesting number for delaying 3 hp by a turn. 6 for 6 in 4 turns is clearly much too weak as +1 to delay 4 is quite weak in most cases and +1 to delay 5 is even worse. 6 for 6 in 3 turns is really the only option and, even then, it would be much more powerful than engineer.

To combat its power as much as possible, I would think the lifespan should be as low as 1. This makes the energy production totally useless in openings. P2 would be giving up a conduit and a green for a drone if they buy one on the first turn in a 3 engi build.

[2019-10-05 21:13] zera777 (Zera): Some thoughts about strategy/balance: Almost every important decision in Prismata is some form of 
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divide_and_choose> The cake-cutting problem. If you cut unevenly, I take the bigger slice (advantage).
and/or
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goldilocks_principle> "Not too much, not too little. Unless the resource isn't granular enough; then tough luck."

<http://blog.prismata.net/2015/06/10/balancing-prismata-openings-part-3/>
Here Elyot explains that when timing a rush, you should buy attackers not when it's advantageous, but as soon as it's not losing (50/50 advantage against an optimal opponent). One of the balance issues in Prismata is making sure that half a turn's difference isn't too large, otherwise, one player can get an advantage the other can't match. Apparently Odin used to have this problem.

<https://prismatalibrary.blog/2018/01/04/iv-defending-against-threat-with-gambits/>
Here 307th explains why it's often bad to fully defend against chill. If you defend fully, the opponent will not use the chill, and now you're behind on gold. If you don't defend enough, they will use it and kill your valuable backline units. But if you say "You can spend ALL your chill to breach me for a single Tarsier", the opponent has a harder choice, closer to 50/50.

The biggest early decision is how many drones to build—not too many, not too few.

For these reasons, I believe high-econ games may be inherently more balanced and strategic than low-econ games. Simply put, the more granular your control is, the easier it is to slice the cake 50/50. And I think, "What if you actually *could* buy half a drone? What if all units/resources could be subdivided arbitrarily?" Perhaps it'd be easier to ensure player balance this way.
